# Questions in Livestream 214

Live Stream #214: Crud-Slurping Egyptian Deities

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=724" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:04</a> | Why does the creation account avoid calling them the sun and moon? | He says those names were tied to other ancient deities, so Genesis avoids them to stress that these lights are just created things under God. | He identifies "sun" and "moon" with deity names in surrounding Levantine cultures and says Genesis avoids that vocabulary so the account is not read as a divine struggle. The point is that the lights are part of creation and are subordinate to God, not rival divine powers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=847" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:07</a> | What do you make of the similarities between Sargon's birth story and Moses? | He says the overlap is mostly the basket motif, while the rest of the stories are quite different. | He pushes back that the stories are only similar at one major point: both infants are placed in baskets connected with a river. He contrasts Sargon being sent down a dangerous river and rescued by a gardener with Moses being placed among the reeds near the Nile, where he is not simply abandoned to drift away. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=1169" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:29</a> | What tips or resources would you recommend for switching to a mainly keto diet? | He recommends removing carbs, relying on fats and meats, and keeping salty broth or stock on hand for the early adjustment period. | His practical advice is to go "cold turkey" by getting rid of potatoes, rice, pasta, sugar, bread, and similar carbs. He describes shifting toward fats and fattier meats, avoiding very lean options, and using salty broth or stock during the initial adjustment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=1594" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:34</a> | What is your favorite ancient Near Eastern subject? | He says he likes practical material culture subjects such as ritual furniture, craftsmanship, gemology, and how things were made. | He does not name one single favorite, but says he finds ritual furniture, temples, ancient gemology, and craft production especially interesting. The common thread is material culture and the practical question of how ancient objects were made. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=1661" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:41</a> | Why is there so much confusion about the Nephilim? | He says later readers lost the late Bronze Age context, and later interpretive traditions filled the gap in different ways. | He argues that the Nephilim belong to a late Bronze Age context that was already being forgotten by later periods. Once that context was lost, later readers supplied new causes and interpretive frameworks, especially in the Second Temple period, which produced more elaborate mythological readings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=1755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:15</a> | Did the Late Bronze Age collapse help people forget what the Nephilim were? | He says that kind of cultural collapse likely contributed, along with broader social and intellectual disruption. | He says the collapse likely contributed, but was not the only factor. The breakdown of large empires, rise of city-states, and shift toward ordinary Iron Age agricultural life made the older conquest-era and imperial context increasingly remote. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=1965" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:45</a> | What makes a good reader or a bad reader? | He reframes the issue as attentive versus inattentive reading, arguing that good readers let the text speak instead of reading themselves into it. | He says "good" and "bad" reader are less helpful categories than attentive and inattentive reader. An attentive reader tries to draw meaning and subtext from the text itself, while an inattentive reader imports personal context and reads themselves into the story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=2228" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:08</a> | What is a favorite story moment that shows character growth? | He points to Dorothy in *The Wizard of Oz* reaching the realization that others are not going to save her, which marks a genuine shift in self-understanding. | He frames character growth as a movement from a false belief to a realization point. After discussing the idea generally, he uses Dorothy as an example: her growth comes when she realizes she has been waiting for others to solve her problem and must take responsibility herself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=2693" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:53</a> | Why does Yehoshua become Yeshua instead of following the usual Yahu to Yo pattern? | He says he does not know of a clear reason and treats it as one of those irregular developments ancient languages sometimes preserve. | He says Yehoshua and Yeshua are the same name, but some forms syncopated around the centuries near the common era. He explicitly says he does not know why this happened and treats it as the sort of irregular development ancient languages often preserve. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=2949" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:09</a> | In Deuteronomy 27:6, why must the altar use uncut stones? | He says the point is that the altar should remain natural and God-made rather than being shaped into a human craft object. | He reads the rule as requiring a natural altar made from stones as God made them. Cut stones would introduce human craftsmanship into the altar, while uncut stones avoid that alteration and keep the altar from being treated as a humanly manufactured sacred object. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=3079" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:19</a> | In Deuteronomy 25:11, what is the issue with grabbing the genitals? | He says it is treated as a serious attack on a man's reproductive future and household continuity, not as trivial accidental contact. | He stresses that the verb means seizing, not merely touching, and interprets the act as a deliberate crushing attack. He also compares Mesopotamian laws, where he says the penalty could be harsher, and explains the issue as an assault on reproduction and family continuity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=3314" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:14</a> | In Deuteronomy 25:3, is the number forty meant literally? | He says the main point is setting a strict maximum so punishment does not become excessive and degrading. | He reads the forty lashes as the maximum allowed punishment, whether or not the question is pressed as literal arithmetic. The law limits the beating so it remains punishment rather than killing or degrading the offender beyond measure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=3440" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:20</a> | Why is Jesus called the Lion of Judah? | He ties it to royal lion imagery and to the Judah blessing tradition in Genesis 49. | He explains that lions functioned as symbols of royalty and kingship across the ancient Near East, including royal throne imagery. Jesus as the Lion of Judah therefore signals kingship from Judah and connects with the Genesis 49 blessing tradition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=3655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:55</a> | Was Baal-Hadad really a fertility god? | He says Baal-Hadad is primarily a storm god, though storms make him indirectly relevant to agricultural fertility through rain. | He distinguishes Baal-Hadad's main role as storm god from fertility in a narrower sense. Because controlling storms and rain affects crops, Baal becomes indirectly connected with fertility, but the core identity is storm control rather than being primarily a fertility deity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=3734" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:14</a> | Did ancient Egyptians use wedding rings? | He says he does not know evidence for that in ancient Egypt and points instead to later Roman evidence for wedding rings. | He says popular histories often claim Egyptian origins for wedding rings, but he does not know real Egyptian evidence from tombs, mummies, or other sources. The earliest evidence he knows is Roman, especially rings associated with senators' wives and a key motif showing household authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=3953" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:53</a> | In Deuteronomy 33:5, is the he Moses, Jacob, or God? | He says the context points to the Lord rather than Moses or Jacob. | After reading the surrounding blessing, he says the immediate context makes the Lord the most likely referent. He also notes that Jeshurun is generally taken as a synonym for Israel, which makes Moses or Jacob less likely in that slot. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=4072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:52</a> | What is Jeshurun? | He describes it as a title or synonym for Israel. | He gives a brief answer and says most commentators understand Jeshurun as a synonym or title for Israel. He presents that as his best understanding rather than developing a long separate argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=4102" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:22</a> | In Deuteronomy 33:13-18, why is Joseph treated like the firstborn? | He says Joseph receives firstborn-style privilege because Reuben was disqualified, Joseph preserved the family, and Jacob especially favored him. | He first qualifies the wording, saying Joseph is described as distinguished rather than simply firstborn. He then connects Joseph's elevated position with Reuben's disqualification, Joseph's role in preserving the family, Jacob's preference for Joseph, and the way Ephraim and Manasseh receive prominence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=4614" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:54</a> | In Deuteronomy 32:51, what does it mean that Moses broke faith and did not treat God as holy? | He says Moses misrepresented God's character at Meribah by acting angrily and making the episode about himself rather than sanctifying God before the people. | He connects the verse to Meribah-Kadesh, where Moses struck the rock in anger when the people asked for water. His interpretation is that Moses made the episode about his own frustration and misrepresented what God was communicating, so God was not sanctified before Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=4899" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:39</a> | Can you make a video comparing Abraham and the Code of Hammurabi? | He says yes, probably by adapting work he has already done on the subject. | He says the topic is within range because he already has academic work on Abraham and Hammurabi's code. He suggests that existing paper could probably be converted into a video script. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=4936" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:16</a> | Are you going to make a video on Elisha and the bears? | He treats it as possible in theory and jokes about doing it as a more animated or playful project, but nothing concrete is planned. | The answer becomes playful rather than a firm production commitment. He imagines an animated or parody-style treatment and jokes through possible versions, but the transcript does not give a concrete plan or schedule for such a video. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=5382" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:42</a> | What was the purpose and process of levirate marriage, and would it have been seen as incest? | He says it preserved clan inheritance by having an unmarried brother raise an heir for a deceased brother, and it was not treated as incest. | He says the question cites the wrong verse but answers the institution anyway. Levirate marriage is about clan integrity and inheritance: if a married brother dies childless, the unmarried brother has a clan obligation to marry the widow and produce an heir for the deceased brother's line. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=5888" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:08</a> | Why was Apophis given vulgar or demeaning names? | He says Apophis was the hostile serpent enemy of the sun and cosmic order, so insulting names fit his role as a hated adversary. | He explains that Apophis was imagined as a serpent or demon who tried to devour the sun, which would threaten life, light, and cosmic order. Because he was a hostile figure and possible soul-eating demon, degrading names fit how Egyptians viewed him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=6052" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:52</a> | Why did Ra need help defeating Apophis? Was Apophis that powerful? | He says the tradition is not mainly about Ra being helpless, and he notes that Bastet's cat-against-snake role makes her a fitting slayer in those scenes. | He says it is not really that Ra needed help because Apophis was too powerful. The scene gives Bastet a fitting role because cats and snakes are natural enemies, so a cat deity with a knife is an appropriate mythic opponent for a snake demon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=6150" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:30</a> | What is the weirdest Egyptian mythological creature you have seen? | He mentions a four-headed, four-legged serpent depicted in tomb material. | He answers with a serpent shown in tomb material that has four heads and, as he recalls it, four legs. He treats that as probably the strangest Egyptian mythological creature he has seen. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=6225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:45</a> | Is Joshua Bronze Age literature? | He says no, although he thinks some earlier Pentateuchal material may preserve late Bronze Age roots. | He says Joshua and Judges are not Bronze Age literature because their narrative setting belongs in the Iron Age. He allows that some core Pentateuchal material may preserve late Bronze Age roots, while Joshua and Judges are more plausibly late Iron Age or divided-monarchy era didactic literature, though he does not claim a final date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=6348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:48</a> | If Exodus 12:12 says God judged Egypt's gods, was Yahweh contending with them in the plagues? | He says that verse refers specifically to the Passover judgment and does not require reading every plague as a direct god-versus-god contest. | He reads Exodus 12:12 carefully and says the verse speaks about the Passover night, not necessarily every plague as a direct judgment on a specific Egyptian deity. He warns against overreading one statement into the entire plague sequence and turning it into a broad god-versus-god scheme. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=6897" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:57</a> | Is it true that no other ancient Near Eastern code forbids same-sex relationships like the Torah? | He says no other law code clearly forbids it, but Egyptian material still treats it as outside proper conduct, and Egypt did not have law codes in the same way. | He says the claim is partly true because he does not know another ancient Near Eastern law code with the same prohibition. But he distinguishes law codes from moral disapproval, noting Egyptian negative-confession material that treats the act as something a good Egyptian would deny, even though Egypt did not use law codes like Mesopotamia. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=7038" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:18</a> | What is a modern equivalent of Ananias and Sapphira lying to the Holy Spirit, and would the punishment be the same? | He compares it to performative generosity done for status while secretly holding back, and says the same punishment probably would not apply because Acts describes the fragile early church. | He sets the story in the context of Acts 4, where believers sell property to care for needy Christians, and contrasts Barnabas's generosity with Ananias and Sapphira's deception. The modern equivalent would be pretending total generosity for reputation while secretly withholding part of it, though he treats the death judgment as tied to the unique early-church moment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=7784" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:44</a> | What do you think about millionaire pastors and televangelists? | He says televangelism often creates incentives for money-grabbing, while personal wealth has to be judged by how it was acquired and used. | He separates the medium from the abuse: televangelism is not inherently evil, but television ministry was extremely expensive and therefore often rewarded fundraising-heavy and prosperity-style ministries. He says the moral question about wealthy pastors depends on how the money was gained and used, not merely on the existence of wealth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=8820" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:00</a> | Is there enough evidence to trace chariot technology from the steppe southward into Egypt before the Hyksos period? | He says yes, while dating chariot evidence earlier in the Near East and pointing to horse, bit, bridle, wheel, and foreign-technology evidence in Egypt by the Hyksos period. | He says chariot evidence appears earlier than the question suggests, pointing to early Near Eastern reliefs and wheel evidence, while steppe material contributes riding gear such as bits and bridles. For Egypt, he cites horse mouth deformation from bits, horse breeding, bit and bridle remains, chariot wheels, horse burials, and Hyksos-era foreign technology as enough to trace the movement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=9043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:43</a> | Does the phrase the heavens and the earth support taking Genesis 1:1 as an independent clause? | He says no, because he treats Genesis 1:1 as a heading or summary and sees verse 2 as continuing the same world-setting context. | He is unsure that "the heavens and the earth" must mean the entire world in the way the question assumes. His own reading is that Genesis 1:1 functions as a title or summary rather than a separate creative act, so verse 2 continues the same setting rather than proving an independent-clause reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=9259" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:19</a> | Does Luke 24:27 argue against a christotelic reading of the Old Testament? | He says no, and he thinks the passage can fit a context-first christotelic approach rather than forcing a flatter christocentric reading. | He says Luke 24:27 is compatible with both christocentric and christotelic readings. He distinguishes the two by saying a christocentric reading starts by finding Christ in every Old Testament text, while a christotelic reading starts with the Old Testament context and then moves toward Christ as the goal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=9578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:38</a> | How certain are we about the Tel Dan inscription, and could it be a forgery? | He says it is not a forgery and regards the objections as weak because the inscription was excavated in context. | He rejects the forgery objection, especially the argument that the inscription is too clear or too good to be real. His main point is that the Tel Dan stele was found archaeologically in the dirt and under strata, so the context weighs strongly against forgery claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=9813" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:33</a> | Which people group is older, Egyptians or Iranians? | He says the answer depends on what counts as Iranian, but Persians are later than Egyptians. | He says "Egyptians" is a clearer category because Egypt was a more monolithic culture with a definable start and historical span. "Iranian" is less clear: if the question means Persians, Egyptians are older, but if it includes groups such as Elamites, the answer becomes much less straightforward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=9939" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:39</a> | In Genesis 17:17, was Abraham being hyperbolic about his and Sarah's age? | He says yes in some sense, while noting that the story still presents them as truly beyond ordinary childbearing expectation. | He says hyperbole is possible, but the passage still depends on Abraham and Sarah being beyond normal childbearing expectation. He notes the text presents Abraham laughing at the idea of a child at that age, and later comments reinforce that they were effectively past childbearing years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=10322" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:02</a> | Which verse explicitly says Abraham was a clan rather than just one individual? | He says no single verse states that outright; it is an inference drawn from the broader narrative and genealogical presentation. | He says the clan reading is an inference, not a single proof text. He supports it by pointing to tensions in the age statements and genealogies, such as Abraham being surprised about fatherhood at one hundred despite other patriarchal ages and later offspring, and to rapid population claims that do not work well if read as only a few individual generations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=11342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:02</a> | Why are conspiracy theories so addictive, and has academia failed to bridge the ivory tower gap? | He says conspiracy theories can give people an anti-establishment thrill, and he agrees academia often fails to communicate accessibly. | He says academia often fails to explain its work outside disciplinary silos, which leaves room for outsiders to misunderstand or distrust experts. He later explains conspiracy theories as addictive because anti-establishment claims can give people an immediate sense of pleasure or excitement from feeling that the official account has been overturned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=11701" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:01</a> | How does ChatGPT decide whether something is boring? | He says it does not really know boredom but infers likely reactions from patterns in the material it has seen. | He says ChatGPT is a computer and does not know boredom directly. In his example, it inferred from an academic author's reputation and limited review material rather than actually judging the content, which he uses as an illustration of broader assumptions about academic writing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=12115" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:55</a> | In Numbers 13, do the spies think their God cannot overcome the inhabitants? | He says the text explicitly shows fear of the people, while any doubt about God is at most an implication rather than a direct statement. | He looks to the end of Numbers 13 and says the spies explicitly say the inhabitants are too strong for them. Since the passage does not directly mention God or Yahweh in that complaint, he treats doubt about God as possible background but not something stated outright. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=12233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:53</a> | Do we know which arguments persuaded ancient Greek philosophers to become Christians? | He says the draw was often less formal apologetic argument and more the imaginative, literary, and religious world opened by scriptural and Christian texts. | He says some Greek philosophers were not primarily persuaded by formal apologetics. He points instead to Greek poets, mythology, ritual, worship, and poetry influenced by the Septuagint as important pathways that made Christian claims intelligible or compelling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=12475" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:55</a> | Is part of the Abraham story showing that the Lord is not just a local territorial god? | He says yes, reading Abraham's movements as part of a shift away from purely local divine geography toward a more universal conception. | He says Bronze Age religion was strongly local, with gods tied to territories. The Abraham stories, as preserved in a late Bronze Age to early Iron Age context, show a transition away from that localism toward a broader and more universal understanding of God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=12659" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:30:59</a> | Why would God give humans freedom to sin and suffer, and how is that not his responsibility? | He says genuine freedom requires real choice rather than robotic obedience, while acknowledging that the larger problem of evil is still difficult. | He answers by distinguishing primary and secondary causes: God gives the conditions and freedom, but a person chooses what to do within that freedom. He uses the analogy of a king who makes laws but does not force a subject to break them, while still admitting the broader issue remains difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=13154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:14</a> | What is the religious makeup of biblical scholars, and why do they often differ from orthodox Christian views? | He says the field is mixed and that any simple consensus map breaks down because institutions, subfields, incentives, and gatekeeping all vary. | He says biblical studies includes progressive Christians, atheists, orthodox Christians, and others, so it cannot be reduced to one affiliation. Because the field is large and fragmented, claims about consensus often depend on networks, institutions, and whom scholars tend to interact with. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=13532" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:32</a> | Should some biblical verses be renumbered or broken up differently? | He says yes, especially for some chapter divisions and spacing decisions, since those later editorial aids are not inspired and are sometimes clumsy. | He says the issue is especially clear with chapter breaks, and gives Genesis 1 continuing into Genesis 2:4 as an example. He also notes that chapter breaks, verse breaks, and even later word spacing are not inspired in the same way as Scripture itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=13860" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:00</a> | Can you talk about the weirdest verse in the Bible? | He declines to pick a single winner and argues that many verses only seem weird because readers miss their context. | He considers several examples but says many passages that seem strange become understandable once their cultural context is known. His main answer is that he cannot confidently identify a single weirdest verse, because much of the perceived weirdness comes from modern distance from the ancient world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=14443" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:00:43</a> | Is anyone still really practicing ancient Egyptian religion? | He says no one is practicing the original form, because the ancient religion depended on a temple-state system and other conditions that no longer exist. | He says people may claim to practice ancient Egyptian religion, but the original system required a state-supported temple cult, major expense, special materials, daily priestly labor, ritual facilities, and practices that would be illegal today. For that reason, modern attempts are not the ancient system itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sQAT3gBSn5c?t=14804" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:06:44</a> | Have you thought about adding more channel emoji options? | He says he has considered it a little, but it has not been a priority yet. | He says he has thought about additional emojis and noticed there may be room for a few more, but exhaustion and other work have kept it from becoming a priority. He also notes the tiny icon size limits how detailed an emoji can be. |
